content:
Java

Programming

Software Development

Asynchronous Programming

Threads

Part 1: Threads: Fundamentals

8 min read
Just now

--

Before you’re handed any tool, understand the raw material
1. A Checkout That Shouldn’t Take 800ms
Picture a checkout API. It does four things, one after another:

• Validate the order — 50ms (in-memory)

• Charge the payment — 300ms (network call to a payment gateway)

• Reserve inventory — 250ms (network call to inventory service)

• Notify the user — 200ms (network call to notification service)
Written the obvious way, in a single thread, top to bottom:
public OrderResult checkout(Order order) {
validate(order); // 50ms
chargePayment(order); // 300ms
reserveInventory(order); // 250ms
notifyUser(order); // 200ms
return OrderResult.success();
}Total: 800ms, every single time — even though charging payment and reserving inventory don’t depend on each other and could happen at the same time. Worse: while that thread is waiting 300ms for the payment gateway to respond, it isn’t doing anything. It’s just sitting there, blocked, holding memory, unable to serve any other request.
Scale that to 500 concurrent checkouts and you’ll either run out of threads or run out of memory, because — as we’re about to see — threads are not free.
It’ll be tempting to look this up and reach for a fix in five minutes. Resist that for now. Everything that makes the eventual fix actually safe, instead of just fast in a demo, starts with a question most tutorials skip past: what is a thread, actually, in terms that don’t require already knowing the answer?

2. What Is a Thread, Really?
Forget the textbook definition for a second. Picture a restaurant.
The restaurant itself — the building, the address, the equipment — is your process: a running program with its own private slice of memory and OS resources. Inside that restaurant, you don’t have one person doing every job. You have several chefs working at the same time — one stirring soup, one watching the oven, one plating dessert. Each chef is a thread: an independent line of execution, working through its own list of steps, inside the same process.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here’s the detail that matters more than the analogy itself: all three chefs share the same pantry and the same fridge. If Chef 1 grabs the salt, moves it, or uses it up, every other chef sees that change — because it’s not their salt, it’s the restaurant’s salt. In Java terms: every thread in a process shares the same heap memory. That sharing is not a footnote — it’s the entire reason concurrency bugs exist. Two threads can read and write the exact same object at the exact same time, and nothing about that is automatically safe. We’re not going to prove that yet — just remember it, because it’s going to come back.
Now the textbook definition should actually land: a thread is the smallest unit of execution inside a process. A process can contain many threads, and they all execute independently while sharing that process’s memory.
Concurrency vs. parallelism — not the same thing
These two words get used interchangeably, and they shouldn’t be:

Press enter or click to view image in full size

• Concurrency — one chef, switching between stirring the soup, checking the oven, and chopping vegetables, fast enough that it looks like all three are happening at once. Only one thing is actually happening at any single instant — the CPU is time-slicing between tasks.

• Parallelism — three chefs, three tasks, genuinely happening at the same physical instant, because there are three sets of hands (three CPU cores) instead of one.
A single-core machine can still give you concurrency — the OS just switches between threads fast enough that it feels simultaneous. It can never give you true parallelism; for that you need multiple cores actually executing at once. This distinction matters for the rest of this series: async programming is fundamentally about concurrency. It helps even on a single core, because the win isn’t “do two things at the exact same instant” — it’s “don’t sit idle doing nothing while waiting on one of them.”

3. Creating a Thread — With Actual Output
Enough analogy. Here’s a real, runnable example — two threads, both running the exact same task, at the exact same time:
public class ThreadDemo {
public static void main(String[] args) {
Runnable printNumbers = () -> {
for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " -> " + i);
}
}; Thread chefA = new Thread(printNumbers, "Chef-A");
Thread chefB = new Thread(printNumbers, "Chef-B");
chefA.start();
chefB.start();
}
}
Run this a few times and you’ll notice something: the output isn’t the same twice.
Chef-A -> 1
Chef-B -> 1
Chef-A -> 2
Chef-B -> 2
Chef-A -> 3
Chef-B -> 3Chef-B -> 1
Chef-A -> 1
Chef-B -> 2
Chef-A -> 2
Chef-A -> 3
Chef-B -> 3Both are valid outputs of the exact same program. chefA.start() and chefB.start() don't mean "run A, then run B" — they mean "both of these are now eligible to run, and the OS scheduler decides the interleaving." That unpredictability isn't a bug in this example — it's the single most important fact about threads to internalize before writing concurrent code: you don't control the order. Code that happens to work in a particular order during testing can and will produce a different order in production, under load, on a different machine.
There are two ways to actually create a thread, and only one of them you should use:
// Way 1: extend Thread — couples your task to the threading mechanism. Avoid this.
class MyThread extends Thread {
public void run() {
System.out.println("Running");
}
}// Way 2: implement Runnable — preferred. The task is just a task; anything can run it.
Runnable task = () -> System.out.println("Running");
Thread t = new Thread(task);
t.start();Extending Thread ties your business logic to "being a thread." A Runnable is just a piece of work — it can be run directly or handed to a thread. Keep that distinction — a task is not the same thing as a thread — in the back of your mind; it matters more the deeper into this series you get.
There’s a sibling to Runnable worth knowing: Callable.
FeatureRunnableCallable<V>Methodrun()call()Returns a valueNoYesCan throw checked exceptionsNoYes
If your task needs to produce a result or might fail with a checked exception — Callable. If it's fire-and-forget — Runnable.

4. The Lifecycle of a Thread
A thread moves through a strict set of states, and it only ever moves forward:

Press enter or click to view image in full size

• NEW — created with new Thread(...), not yet started. In the restaurant, this is a chef who's been hired but hasn't clocked in yet.

• RUNNABLE — start() was called; it's eligible to run, waiting for the OS scheduler. Clocked in, standing in the kitchen, waiting for the head chef to point at a station.

• RUNNING — actually executing on a CPU right now. Actively cooking.

• BLOCKED / WAITING — paused, waiting for a lock, a notify(), I/O, or join(). Standing at the walk-in fridge, waiting for the chef currently inside it to come out.

• TERMINATED — run() has returned. The thread object is now dead — it cannot be restarted, not even by calling start() again. Clocked out for good.
That last point trips people up constantly: a Thread is a single-use object. Once it terminates, you throw it away. Which raises an obvious question — if I need to run 10,000 short tasks over the life of my application, am I supposed to hire and fire 10,000 chefs?

5. The Problem With Manual Threads
Creating a raw Thread for every unit of work sounds simple, and it works fine in a toy example. It falls apart in a real system for concrete, measurable reasons:

• Thread creation is expensive. Each Thread maps to an OS-level thread with its own stack (typically 512KB–1MB by default on the JVM). Creating one isn't a cheap object allocation — it's a system call. Hiring a chef takes paperwork, not just a name tag.

• No reuse. A terminated thread is garbage. Every new task means paying the creation cost again — hiring a brand new chef for every single dish, then firing them the moment it’s plated.

• Lifecycle management is on you. Tracking which threads are alive, waiting for them to finish, handling their exceptions — all manual, all error-prone.

• Unbounded thread creation risks the process itself. If traffic spikes and you spin up a thread per request with no ceiling, you can exhaust OS thread limits or run out of memory (thousands of 1MB stacks add up fast) and crash the JVM — not just slow it down. A restaurant that hires a new chef for every single customer runs out of kitchen space long before it runs out of customers.

• Concurrency is hard to control. Nothing stops 5,000 threads from all hitting a downstream database at once and taking it down — 5,000 chefs all reaching into the same small fridge at once.
None of these are theoretical. “Thread per request” was a common architecture in early Java web servers, and it’s a large part of why they fell over under load.
And there’s a fifth problem, more dangerous than any of these, and it’s the one the two-chef example back in section 3 was quietly setting up: the moment two threads share data — that same shared pantry — everything you think you know about “the code just runs top to bottom” stops being reliable. A write one thread makes isn’t guaranteed to be seen by another. A read-modify-write isn’t guaranteed to be atomic just because it looks like one line of code. These aren’t rare edge cases. They’re the default behavior of shared mutable state across threads — and once you’ve seen exactly how that breaks, “just wrap it in a thread pool” stops sounding like a complete answer.

6. The Mental Model to Keep
A thread is a chef sharing a kitchen — an independent line of execution inside a process, free to run at its own pace, but sharing every ingredient in the pantry with every other chef in the building. You don’t control the order they work in. You do control what they share and how carefully.
That sharing is where this gets interesting.
